agent_output: {"integration_assessment":{"component_interactions":[{"from":"client","to":"upload_service","status":"correct","issue":"Accepts uploads up to 50MB with format validation"},{"from":"upload_service","to":"s3","status":"risk","issue":"S3 failures are not broken out from general request failures. When S3 has a regional degradation, on-call cannot distinguish S3-specific errors from validation failures or other request errors without grepping logs. This delays incident response."},{"from":"upload_service","to":"prometheus","status":"risk","issue":"No latency histogram means the 10s SLA (p99 < 10s) is unmeasurable from metrics alone. The team cannot alert on SLA breach or track SLA compliance over time without manual log analysis."}],"contract_compliance":"File size limit (50MB) and format validation are present. SLA of p99 < 10s is defined but not instrumentally verifiable.","data_flow_integrity":"Request ID propagation in structured logs is good. Data flow from client → service → S3 is straightforward. No async processing or queues involved, reducing integration risk."},"observability_review":{"logging":{"coverage":"adequate","gaps":["Verify that S3 error responses (status code, error code, request ID from AWS) are logged — needed to file AWS support tickets","Confirm retry attempts are logged individually so failed-then-succeeded uploads are distinguishable from first-attempt successes"]},"metrics":{"coverage":"gaps","gaps":["MUST-HAVE: No latency histogram (e.g. upload_duration_seconds with buckets). Without this, the p99 < 10s SLA cannot be monitored or alerted on. This is the single biggest operational gap.","MUST-HAVE: No S3-specific error rate metric. requests_failed_total lumps validation errors, S3 errors, and timeouts together. On-call cannot isolate root cause from a dashboard.","NICE-TO-HAVE: No file size distribution metric (histogram). Understanding upload size patterns helps capacity planning and correlating latency spikes with large files.","NICE-TO-HAVE: No in-flight request gauge. Under load, knowing concurrent upload count helps diagnose resource exhaustion."]},"tracing":{"coverage":"gaps","gaps":["No mention of distributed tracing. If this service is called by other services or triggers downstream processing, trace context propagation is needed. Low priority if this is a standalone endpoint."]},"debugging_capability":"Structured JSON logging with request ID, file size, and duration is solid for log-based debugging. The gap is in real-time dashboarding — an on-call engineer cannot answer 'are we meeting SLA right now?' without running a log query."},"resilience_assessment":{"failure_modes":[{"scenario":"S3 regional outage or degradation","handling":"partial","recommendation":"2 retries help with transient errors but not sustained outages. Add a circuit breaker or at minimum a metric + alert on S3 error rate so the team knows immediately. Consider returning a specific 503 with Retry-After header so clients back off."},{"scenario":"Client uploads 50MB file on slow connection, S3 upload times out at 30s","handling":"partial","recommendation":"30s timeout on S3 is reasonable for 50MB, but there is no mention of a client-facing request timeout. If the client-to-service connection has no timeout, a slow upload could hold a connection indefinitely. Ensure the HTTP server has an overall request timeout (e.g., 60s) to bound resource usage."},{"scenario":"Burst of large file uploads exhausts memory or connections","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"No mention of concurrency limits or request body streaming. If the service buffers entire 50MB files in memory, 20 concurrent uploads = 1GB RAM. Ensure uploads are streamed to S3 (multipart) rather than buffered, or enforce concurrency limits."},{"scenario":"S3 upload succeeds but response is lost (network blip after PUT)","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"Client retries, file is uploaded twice. This is acceptable if uploads are idempotent (keyed by client-provided ID). Verify idempotency strategy."},{"scenario":"Disk or temp storage fills up if files are staged locally","handling":"unhandled","recommendation":"If files are written to /tmp before S3 upload, a burst could fill disk. Confirm whether files are streamed directly to S3 or staged locally."}],"retry_config":"2 retries on S3 failures. Acceptable for transient errors. Confirm exponential backoff is used — fixed-interval retries during S3 throttling will make things worse.","timeout_config":"30s on S3 calls. Reasonable for 50MB uploads. Missing: overall HTTP request timeout on the server side to prevent connection exhaustion from slow clients.","circuit_breakers":"None. For a single-dependency service (S3), a circuit breaker is nice-to-have, not must-have. The retry + timeout combination is sufficient if S3 error rate alerting is added.","graceful_degradation":"None described. If S3 is down, the service fails all uploads. Consider: could uploads be queued to a local buffer and retried? For most use cases, fast-failing with a clear error is the right call — but the client needs a clear signal (503 + Retry-After)."},"operational_readiness":{"deployment_ready":false,"rollback_capable":true,"rollback_plan":"Redeploy previous container image. Acceptable for a stateless service. Verify that no database migrations or S3 bucket policy changes are coupled to deployment.","monitoring_coverage":"Insufficient. requests_total and requests_failed_total provide basic availability signal but cannot answer: (1) Are we meeting SLA? (2) Is S3 specifically degraded? (3) What does our latency distribution look like? The /health endpoint checking S3 connectivity is good for load balancer integration.","on_call_documentation":"Not mentioned. Needs a runbook covering: how to check SLA compliance, what to do when S3 errors spike, how to identify and kill stuck uploads, how to verify the service is healthy beyond /health.","new_infrastructure_required":{"new_env_vars":[],"new_services":[],"new_queues_topics":[],"config_changes":["Add Prometheus alerting rules for: SLA breach (p99 latency > 10s), elevated S3 error rate, elevated overall error rate"],"infra_provisioning":["Grafana dashboard for upload latency, error rates by type, file size distribution, and request volume"]},"database_safety":{"has_migrations":false,"reversible":false,"data_preserving":true,"large_table_impact":false,"details":"No database involved. S3 is the only data store."},"verdict":"NOT READY"},"escalations":[{"type":"missing_observability","issue":"No latency histogram metric makes the p99 < 10s SLA unmonitorable","reason":"The service has a defined SLA (99% of uploads < 10s) but no instrument to measure it. Without upload_duration_seconds histogram, on-call cannot detect SLA breaches from dashboards or set alerts. This is the equivalent of shipping with no speedometer and a speed limit. Adding a histogram is a small code change (a few lines) and must be done before production.","routed_to":"Coach K"},{"type":"missing_observability","issue":"S3 errors not broken out from general failures","reason":"When requests_failed_total spikes at 3am, on-call cannot tell if S3 is down vs. clients sending bad files. A single label (e.g., error_type=s3|validation|timeout) on the failure counter, or a dedicated s3_errors_total metric, is required for effective incident response. Without it, MTTR increases significantly.","routed_to":"Coach K"}],"confidence":{"level":82,"high_confidence_areas":["Observability gaps are clear and well-defined","Resilience posture (retry + timeout) is reasonable for the architecture","Rollback strategy is sound for a stateless service"],"low_confidence_areas":["Memory/streaming behavior unknown — if files are buffered in memory, there is a significant resource exhaustion risk under load","Client-facing timeout configuration not specified","Idempotency strategy for retried uploads not confirmed"],"assumptions":["Service is stateless and horizontally scalable","S3 is the only external dependency","No database migrations are involved in deployment","Structured logging is going to a centralized log aggregation system"]}}
